title: M01-L004 — Numbers 0 to 10: Age, Room Numbers, and Basic Counting

sections.heading: What you will be able to say after this lesson
body: Target runtime: 9-12 minutes
Scenario: A beginner needs to catch digits in real life, repeat them back clearly, and survive a short number exchange without freezing.
Learner takeaway: By the end, the learner can hear, repeat, and use Thai digits 0-10 in room-number, counting, and simple age practice.

sections.heading: Core phrases (Thai / Transliteration / English)
body: ศูนย์ | sǔun | zero
หนึ่ง | nùeng | one
สอง | sǎawng | two
สาม | sǎam | three
สี่ | sìi | four
ห้า | hâa | five
หก | hòk | six
เจ็ด | jèt | seven
แปด | bpàaet | eight
เก้า | gâao | nine
สิบ | sìp | ten
เบอร์อะไร | ber à-rai | what number?
ฉัน | chǎn | I
เขา | khǎo | he / she
ฉันอายุสิบปี | chǎn aa-yú sìp bpii | I am ten years old
เขาอายุเก้าปี | khǎo aa-yú gâao bpii | he / she is nine years old
ขอโทษ | khǎaw-thôot | excuse me / sorry

sections.heading: Teaching notes and explanation cues
body: Build the first digit map: 0 to 3: Give the learner a clean starting map for zero through three and explain that everyday Thai codes are usually said digit by digit.
Start with the first four digits you will hear all the time: ศูนย์ sǔun, หนึ่ง nùeng, สอง sǎawng, สาม sǎam.
For room numbers, phone numbers, and queue numbers, Thai usually says each digit one by one, not as one big math word.
Add 4 to 7 with pronunciation control: Teach the middle set where beginners often blur vowels or rush final consonants, especially with สี่, ห้า, หก, and เจ็ด.
Now add the middle set: สี่ sìi, ห้า hâa, หก hòk, เจ็ด jèt.
Do not treat these as just English labels with Thai written next to them. Hear the shape of each word: สี่ is long, หก is clipped, and เจ็ด ends sharply.
Finish 8 to 10 and ask for a number: Complete the 0-10 set and introduce เบอร์อะไร as a practical chunk for asking about room numbers, table numbers, and other everyday number labels.
Finish the set with แปด bpàaet, เก้า gâao, and สิบ sìp.
สิบ means ten, but for now the real job is not mental arithmetic. The real job is hearing each digit clearly and saying it back clearly.
Use numbers in short real phrases: Move from isolated digits into usable age and room-number phrases while explicitly reusing the pronoun and identity material from the rebuilt foundation lessons.
Numbers become much more useful when they plug into the people language you already know. From lesson three, you can now drop digits into frames such as ฉันอายุสิบปี or เขาอายุเก้าปี.
You already know pronouns like ฉัน, ผม, คุณ, and เขา. That means numbers are no longer floating on their own; they attach to real people, room numbers, and simple personal facts.

sections.heading: Mini dialogues / role-play
body: A: สวัสดีค่ะ ขอโทษนะคะ คนนี้คือใครคะ | sà-wàt-dii khâ khǎaw-thôot ná khá khon níi kheuu khrai khá | Hello, excuse me, who is this person?
B: คนนี้คือมีนครับ | khon níi kheuu Miin khráp | This is Min.
A: เขาอายุเก้าปีใช่ไหมคะ | khǎo aa-yú gâao bpii châi mái khá | She is nine years old, right?
B: ใช่ครับ | châi khráp | Yes.
A: ห้องเบอร์อะไรคะ | hâawng ber à-rai khá | What is the room number?
B: ห้า-ศูนย์-สองครับ | hâa-sǔun-sǎawng khráp | Five-zero-two.
A: ขอบคุณค่ะ | khàawp-khun khâ | Thank you.

sections.heading: Practice drills (self-study)
body: Listen and repeat: ศูนย์-หนึ่ง-สอง-สาม, then say the same line backwards.
Tutor says one digit; learner points to 0, 1, 2, or 3 and then repeats the Thai word aloud.
Clap once for each digit: สี่-ห้า-หก-เจ็ด, keeping หก and เจ็ด short and controlled.
Minimal-cue drill: teacher says a digit in Thai, learner repeats only that digit without translating first.
Call-and-response: teacher says the Arabic numeral 8, 9, or 10; learner answers in Thai.
Prompt drill: teacher asks เบอร์อะไร and learner answers with a short digit string such as ห้า-ศูนย์-สอง.
Pattern drill: teacher gives a digit from zero to ten; learner builds ฉันอายุ...ปี or เขาอายุ...ปี with that digit.
Repair drill: learner hears a room number once, then must respond with either the number or ขอโทษ before hearing it again and repeating it back.

หนึ่ง | nùeng | one

สี่ | sìi | four

แปด | bpàaet | eight
เก้า | gâao | nine
สิบ | sìp | ten
เบอร์อะไร | ber à-rai | what number?
ฉัน | chǎn | I
เขา | khǎo | he / she
ฉันอายุสิบปี | chǎn aa-yú sìp bpii | I am ten years old
เขาอายุเก้าปี | khǎo aa-yú gâao bpii | he / she is nine years old
ขอโทษ | khǎaw-thôot | excuse me / sorry

sections.heading: Quick recap + memory aids
body: Thai digits are most useful when they are attached to a real beginner job such as a room number or an age.
Reuse pronouns from the previous lesson so numbers describe people, not just abstract counting.
Use อายุ + number + ปี for beginner age practice, then expand the range later.
If you miss a number, recycle ขอโทษ or another repair phrase instead of going silent.

drills: Listen and repeat: ศูนย์-หนึ่ง-สอง-สาม, then say the same line backwards.
Tutor says one digit; learner points to 0, 1, 2, or 3 and then repeats the Thai word aloud.
Clap once for each digit: สี่-ห้า-หก-เจ็ด, keeping หก and เจ็ด short and controlled.
Minimal-cue drill: teacher says a digit in Thai, learner repeats only that digit without translating first.
Call-and-response: teacher says the Arabic numeral 8, 9, or 10; learner answers in Thai.
Prompt drill: teacher asks เบอร์อะไร and learner answers with a short digit string such as ห้า-ศูนย์-สอง.
Pattern drill: teacher gives a digit from zero to ten; learner builds ฉันอายุ...ปี or เขาอายุ...ปี with that digit.
Repair drill: learner hears a room number once, then must respond with either the number or ขอโทษ before hearing it again and repeating it back.
answerKey: Use PTM inline transliteration marks in every practice line.
Thai digits are most useful when they are attached to a real beginner job such as a room number or an age.
Reuse pronouns from the previous lesson so numbers describe people, not just abstract counting.
Use อายุ + number + ปี for beginner age practice, then expand the range later.
If you miss a number, recycle ขอโทษ or another repair phrase instead of going silent.
